title: Professional Communication Skills
obj: Students will be able to demonstrate effective written, verbal, and listening communication skills appropriate for the technology workplace.
content: Written communication

email etiquette, ticket and incident documentation, technical memos, and chat/Slack tone; matching formality to audience (peer, manager, client); clarity, brevity, and correct grammar as professionalism markers
Verbal communication
structuring a status update, explaining technical concepts to non-technical stakeholders, giving and receiving instructions, handling phone and video calls with clients
Active listening
paraphrasing to confirm understanding, taking notes during user interviews, avoiding interruption, asking clarifying questions before responding
Nonverbal cues
eye contact, posture, camera-on expectations on video calls, and reading body language to gauge whether a message is landing
Audience and purpose
adjusting vocabulary and depth for executives vs. engineers vs. end-users; deciding when an email, a meeting, or a quick message is the right channel
Common pitfalls
jargon overload, burying the lede, reply-all misuse, defensive tone in written responses, and the difference between urgency and importance
assess: Students rewrite a poorly worded support email into a professional version and justify each edit
Role-play: one student explains a technical issue verbally while another paraphrases back what they heard; partner scores accuracy
Short-response: list three clarifying questions to ask when a user reports 'the system is broken' and explain what each reveals

title: Creativity and Innovation in the Workplace
obj: Students will be able to demonstrate creativity and innovation by asking meaningful questions and exploring new methods for completing workplace tasks.
content: Curiosity as a skill
asking 'why does this process exist?' and 'what would we do if we started from scratch?'; treating questions as contributions rather than challenges
Divergent vs. convergent thinking
brainstorming broadly before narrowing; deferring judgment during idea generation; using techniques like mind maps and 'five whys'
Innovative work methods
identifying repetitive tasks that could be automated, streamlined, or redesigned; suggesting small process improvements without waiting for permission
Idea pitching
framing an idea with the problem it solves, the proposed change, and the expected benefit; anticipating objections and costs
Learning from failure
treating experiments that don't work as data, documenting what was learned, and iterating rather than abandoning
Innovation and ethics
considering who is affected by a change, whether new methods introduce bias or risk, and balancing novelty with reliability
assess: Idea journal: over one week, students log three workplace or classroom processes they would change and why
Brainstorm exercise: in small groups, generate ten ways to improve a familiar task in three minutes, then pick the most promising and explain the choice
One-minute pitch: students present an innovative idea following problem/proposal/benefit structure to the class

title: Critical Thinking for Career Planning
obj: Students will be able to apply critical-thinking and problem-solving strategies to locate, analyze, and evaluate career and postsecondary information.
content: Structured problem solving
defining the decision, identifying options, gathering evidence, weighing trade-offs, and committing to a next step rather than a forever choice
Locating reliable career information
O*NET, Bureau of Labor Statistics, state labor market data, GeorgiaBEST and GAfutures, professional association sites; distinguishing primary data from marketing copy
Analyzing job postings
reading required vs. preferred qualifications, decoding salary ranges, identifying repeated skills across postings in a target role
Evaluating postsecondary options
cost vs. outcome comparisons for technical college, four-year degree, apprenticeship, and certification paths; total cost including time and opportunity cost
Avoiding reasoning traps
anchoring on the first job seen, confirmation bias, survivorship stories ('my cousin skipped college'), and making long-term decisions from small samples
Decision frameworks
pros-cons lists, weighted criteria matrices, and setting review checkpoints so a plan can be revisited with new information
assess: Career comparison matrix: students analyze three roles in the AI/data pathway across pay, education, growth, and day-to-day tasks using cited sources
Job-posting teardown: given two real postings for the same title, students list what is identical, what differs, and what to infer about each employer
Decision write-up: students draft a one-page plan for their next 24 months with at least two alternative paths and the conditions under which they would switch

title: Work Readiness and Professional Traits
obj: Students will be able to exhibit work-readiness skills including integrity, punctuality, time management, and respect for diversity in professional settings.
content: Integrity and ethics
honesty in reporting progress, admitting mistakes, following licensing and data-privacy rules, and the long-term cost of shortcuts
Punctuality and attendance
arriving prepared for meetings and shifts, communicating early when delays are unavoidable, and understanding how reliability compounds into trust
Time management
prioritizing with urgent/important framing, breaking work into estimable chunks, using calendars and task lists, and protecting focus time
Respect for diversity
working effectively with colleagues of different backgrounds, perspectives, abilities, and communication styles; inclusive language; recognizing bias
Accountability
owning outcomes of your work, following through on commitments, and escalating early when you cannot deliver
Workplace policies
dress code, acceptable-use, harassment and equal-opportunity policies; knowing how to report concerns and who the trusted channels are
assess: Scenario analysis: for five short workplace scenarios (missed deadline, dishonest peer, exclusionary comment), students describe a professional response
Time-log exercise: students track one school day in 30-minute blocks and identify two time-management improvements
Self-assessment rubric: students rate themselves on eight professional traits and set one measurable goal for the semester

title: Teamwork in the Technology Workplace
obj: Students will be able to work both independently and collaboratively as a team member in technology settings, applying skills that contribute to shared deliverables.
content: Independent work skills
self-direction on assigned tasks, knowing when to proceed and when to ask; managing your own blockers; producing work that does not require rework
Team roles
typical tech-team roles including leads, individual contributors, product, design, QA; how responsibilities overlap on small teams
Collaboration tools
shared documents, version control, issue trackers, stand-ups and retrospectives; async vs. synchronous communication norms
Constructive conflict
separating ideas from people, disagreeing without personal attacks, committing to a decision once made even if you argued against it
Giving and receiving feedback
specific and actionable feedback, code and design reviews, receiving critique without defensiveness
Shared accountability
celebrating wins together, sharing credit, addressing failures as a team rather than seeking individual blame
assess: Group project rubric: students complete a two-session team task and rate their own and peers' contributions against a shared rubric
Role-play: mediate a disagreement between two teammates about which technical approach to use; document the resolution process
Reflection: describe one situation where independent work was appropriate and one where teamwork was essential, with reasoning

title: Professional Image and Presentation
obj: Students will be able to present a professional image through appropriate appearance, behavior, and language in workplace and interview contexts.
content: Appearance and dress
industry norms across tech roles (startup casual, business casual, client-facing formal); grooming basics; dressing one level above the daily norm for interviews and presentations
Professional behavior
handshakes and greetings, meeting etiquette, being on time and prepared, phone and device etiquette during meetings
Professional language
avoiding slang and profanity in workplace settings, using precise technical vocabulary, code-switching between peer banter and client conversations
Digital presence
LinkedIn profile basics, GitHub and portfolio hygiene, keeping personal social media content compatible with professional goals
Email and messaging etiquette
subject lines that summarize, signatures, response-time expectations, and how tone reads differently in text than in person
Representing the organization
understanding that once on the job you speak for the employer in emails, posts, and public events; brand and confidentiality awareness
assess: Interview-ready self-check: students submit a photo or description of interview attire with a short explanation of why it fits the role
Digital-footprint audit: review own public social profiles against a professional-image checklist and list three changes
Email rewrite: given an overly casual email to a manager, students rewrite it for tone, clarity, and professionalism

title: Career and Technical Student Organizations Overview
obj: Students will be able to explain the purpose, mission, and goals of Career and Technical Student Organizations (CTSOs) and identify those aligned with the Information Technology pathway.
content: What a CTSO is
a co-curricular student organization that extends CTE classroom learning through competitions, leadership, and service; recognized at local, state, and national levels
Mission and goals
developing technical skills, leadership, citizenship, and career readiness; integrating CTSO activities into course content rather than treating them as extracurriculars
CTSOs for IT and computer science
FBLA (Future Business Leaders of America) and TSA (Technology Student Association) are the primary CTSOs for the Georgia IT pathway; both run technology- and computer-science-focused events
Membership structure
chapter-level officers and advisors, state associations, and national organization; dues, meetings, and typical yearly calendar
Benefits to students
scholarships, recognition, networking with industry and postsecondary partners, resume and college-application credentials
Integration with coursework
CTSO projects that align with class standards, teachers as advisors, and how participation reinforces employability skills covered throughout the course
assess: Compare-and-contrast: students build a table of FBLA and TSA showing mission, relevant IT/CS events, membership cost, and typical activities
Short response: explain in 150 words why a CTSO is considered co-curricular rather than extracurricular
Interest inventory: students rank five CTSO activity types (competition, leadership, service, travel, networking) and justify their top choice

title: CTSO Participation, Competitions, and Leadership
obj: Students will be able to describe CTSO competitive events, leadership development opportunities, and community-service activities, and plan their own participation.
content: Levels of competition
region or district, state conferences, and national conferences; how students qualify to advance and typical timelines across the school year
IT and CS competitive events
coding, cybersecurity, networking, website design, mobile app development, IT fundamentals, and presentation or business-plan events that use technology
Preparing for competition
studying event guidelines, practicing under time, reviewing rubrics, and iterating on a submission with advisor and peer feedback
Leadership development
chapter officer roles, running meetings, parliamentary procedure basics, and leadership academies or institutes offered by state and national organizations
Community service and civic engagement
chapter service projects, partnering with local nonprofits, tech-for-good initiatives such as device refurbishing or digital-literacy tutoring
Planning your year
mapping competitive events, leadership roles, and service hours onto the school calendar; setting goals and tracking progress with your advisor
assess: Event plan: students pick one IT-focused CTSO competitive event, read its guidelines, and outline a preparation timeline with milestones
Leadership reflection: describe an officer role or leadership activity of interest and the three skills it would build
Service proposal: draft a one-page chapter community-service project including goal, audience, tasks, and success measure

title: AI Careers and Pathway Overview
obj: Students will be able to identify career roles across the AI, machine-learning, and data-science pathway, describe salary ranges and educational requirements, and map postsecondary options that support each role.
content: Core AI/ML/DS roles
data analyst, data scientist, machine-learning engineer, AI engineer, MLOps engineer, AI product manager, prompt engineer, and applied-research roles; how responsibilities differ across the stack
Adjacent and supporting roles
data engineer, business intelligence analyst, AI ethicist and policy analyst, annotation and evaluation specialists, and domain experts partnering with AI teams
Salary and market outlook
typical entry, mid, and senior salary bands in the United States and Georgia metro areas; BLS growth projections; regional clusters including Atlanta tech employers
Educational pathways

high-school CTE pathway completion, industry certifications (Microsoft AI-900, AWS AI Practitioner, Google), associate and bachelor degrees in CS/data science/statistics, bootcamps, and graduate study for research roles
Postsecondary options in Georgia
Technical College System of Georgia programs, University System of Georgia institutions with AI/DS programs, dual-enrollment pathways, and registered apprenticeships
Self-alignment

mapping personal interests (math, coding, communication, design, ethics) to roles; building a multi-year portfolio of coursework, projects, certifications, and CTSO accomplishments that signal readiness
assess: Role research: students document three AI/ML/DS roles including duties, required skills, education, certifications, and Georgia salary range with cited sources
Pathway map: students draft a visual map from current grade through first full-time role, listing courses, certifications, CTSO events, and postsecondary steps
Informational interview plan: identify a local professional in a target role and prepare five questions aligned to career decision making
